keyMyths.title: Ragnarök (Norse)
keyMyths.description: The end of the world in Norse mythology, Ragnarök involves a great battle between gods and giants, leading to the destruction of the world, followed by its rebirth and repopulation. This cycle highlights the cyclical nature of existence, where destruction is a necessary precursor to renewal.

keyMyths.title: The Hindu Yugas
keyMyths.description: Hindu cosmology describes time as cyclical, divided into four Yugas (ages): Satya Yuga, Treta Yuga, Dvapara Yuga, and Kali Yuga. Each Yuga is progressively shorter and characterized by a decline in dharma (righteousness), eventually leading to dissolution (pralaya) and the start of a new cycle.

keyMyths.title: The Aztec Five Suns
keyMyths.description: Aztec mythology describes a series of five creations or "Suns," each ruled by a different god and ending in a cataclysmic event. The present era, the Fifth Sun, is believed to be destined for destruction by earthquakes, emphasizing the impermanence of the world and the cyclical pattern of creation and destruction.

keyMyths.title: Egyptian Solar Cycle
keyMyths.description: The daily journey of the sun god Ra across the sky in Egyptian mythology represents a microcosm of the cosmic cycle. Ra's nightly passage through the underworld, battling the serpent Apophis, symbolizes the constant struggle between order and chaos, with Ra's rebirth each morning ensuring the continuation of the world.
keyMyths.source: The Book of the Dead and various temple inscriptions

keyMyths.title: Platonic Great Year
keyMyths.description: Plato's concept of the Great Year describes a vast cosmic cycle in which the stars and planets return to their original positions, leading to the repetition of historical events. This idea reflects a belief in the cyclical nature of time and the recurring patterns of human history.

sources.name: Mircea Eliade, *The Myth of the Eternal Return*
sources.description: A seminal work exploring the concept of cyclical time in various cultures and religions.
sources.title: Mircea Eliade, *The Myth of the Eternal Return*

sources.name: Joseph Campbell, *The Hero with a Thousand Faces*
sources.description: Explores the monomyth, which can be interpreted as a cyclical journey of transformation and return.
sources.title: Joseph Campbell, *The Hero with a Thousand Faces*

sources.name: Marcel Detienne, *The Creation of Mythology*
sources.description: Discusses the construction and interpretation of mythological narratives, including those related to cosmic cycles.
sources.title: Marcel Detienne, *The Creation of Mythology*

sources.name: Jan Assmann, *Death and Salvation in Ancient Egypt*
sources.description: Provides insights into the Egyptian belief in the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, particularly as it relates to the solar cycle.
sources.title: Jan Assmann, *Death and Salvation in Ancient Egypt*

sources.name: David Carrasco, *Religions of Mesoamerica*
sources.description: Offers detailed analysis of Mesoamerican cosmologies, including the cyclical view of time and creation in Aztec and Mayan cultures.
sources.title: David Carrasco, *Religions of Mesoamerica*
